question:
Consider a set $S$ and a binary operation $*$, i.e., for each $a,b\in S$,
$a*b\in S$.  Assume $(a*b)*a=b$ for all $a,b\in S$.  Prove that
$a*(b*a)=b$ for all $a,b\in S$.
solution:
The hypothesis implies $((b*a)*b)*(b*a)=b$ for all $a,b\in S$
(by replacing $a$ by $b*a$), and
hence $a*(b*a)=b$ for all $a,b\in S$ (using $(b*a)*b = a$).

descriptive_long.question: Consider a set $greatset$ and a binary operation $*$, i.e., for each $firstitem,seconditem\in greatset$, $firstitem*seconditem\in greatset$.  Assume $(firstitem*seconditem)*firstitem=seconditem$ for all $firstitem,seconditem\in greatset$.  Prove that $firstitem*(seconditem*firstitem)=seconditem$ for all $firstitem,seconditem\in greatset$.
descriptive_long.solution: The hypothesis implies $((seconditem*firstitem)*seconditem)*(seconditem*firstitem)=seconditem$ for all $firstitem,seconditem\in greatset$ (by replacing $firstitem$ by $seconditem*firstitem$), and hence $firstitem*(seconditem*firstitem)=seconditem$ for all $firstitem,seconditem\in greatset$ (using $(seconditem*firstitem)*seconditem = firstitem$).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: Consider a set $pineapple$ and a binary operation $*$, i.e., for each $landscape,waterfall\in pineapple$, $landscape*waterfall\in pineapple$.  Assume $(landscape*waterfall)*landscape=waterfall$ for all $landscape,waterfall\in pineapple$.  Prove that $landscape*(waterfall*landscape)=waterfall$ for all $landscape,waterfall\in pineapple$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution: The hypothesis implies $((waterfall*landscape)*waterfall)*(waterfall*landscape)=waterfall$ for all $landscape,waterfall\in pineapple$ (by replacing $landscape$ by $waterfall*landscape$), and hence $landscape*(waterfall*landscape)=waterfall$ for all $landscape,waterfall\in pineapple$ (using $(waterfall*landscape)*waterfall = landscape$).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Consider a set $emptiness$ and a binary operation $*$, i.e., for each $nonentity,voidness\in emptiness$,
$nonentity*voidness\in emptiness$.  Assume $(nonentity*voidness)*nonentity=voidness$ for all $nonentity,voidness\in emptiness$.  Prove that
$nonentity*(voidness*nonentity)=voidness$ for all $nonentity,voidness\in emptiness$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The hypothesis implies $((voidness*nonentity)*voidness)*(voidness*nonentity)=voidness$ for all $nonentity,voidness\in emptiness$
(by replacing $nonentity$ by $voidness*nonentity$), and
hence $nonentity*(voidness*nonentity)=voidness$ for all $nonentity,voidness\in emptiness$ (using $(voidness*nonentity)*voidness = nonentity$).

garbled_string.question: Consider a set $qbvdmien$ and a binary operation $*$, i.e., for each $knfjwuza,gvxrelmp\in qbvdmien$, $knfjwuza*gvxrelmp\in qbvdmien$.  Assume $(knfjwuza*gvxrelmp)*knfjwuza=gvxrelmp$ for all $knfjwuza,gvxrelmp\in qbvdmien$.  Prove that $knfjwuza*(gvxrelmp*knfjwuza)=gvxrelmp$ for all $knfjwuza,gvxrelmp\in qbvdmien$.
garbled_string.solution: The hypothesis implies $((gvxrelmp*knfjwuza)*gvxrelmp)*(gvxrelmp*knfjwuza)=gvxrelmp$ for all $knfjwuza,gvxrelmp\in qbvdmien$ (by replacing $knfjwuza$ by $gvxrelmp*knfjwuza$), and hence $knfjwuza*(gvxrelmp*knfjwuza)=gvxrelmp$ for all $knfjwuza,gvxrelmp\in qbvdmien$ (using $(gvxrelmp*knfjwuza)*gvxrelmp = knfjwuza$).

kernel_variant.question:
Let \(\Omega\) be a non-empty set equipped with a binary operation \(\diamond : \Omega\times\Omega\to\Omega\). Assume that for every pair of elements \(p,q\in\Omega\) the identity
\[
   (p\diamond q)\diamond p = q
\]
holds.  Prove that
\[
   p\diamond(q\diamond p)=q
\]
for all \(p,q\in\Omega\).
kernel_variant.solution:
Because the identity
\[(p\diamond q)\diamond p=q\tag{1}\]
is valid for every ordered pair \((p,q)\), we can make the following deductions.

Step 1 (Substitution).  Replace \(p\) in (1) with the composite element \(q\diamond p\).  This is permissible for all \(p,q\in\Omega\), and it yields
\[((q\diamond p)\diamond q)\diamond(q\diamond p)=q.\tag{2}\]

Step 2 (Variable switch).  Interchange the roles of the two variables in (1) by using the pair \((q,p)\) in place of \((p,q)\).  We obtain
\[(q\diamond p)\diamond q=p.\tag{3}\]

Step 3 (Substitution of the intermediate result).  Insert the value of \((q\diamond p)\diamond q\) from (3) into equation (2).  Doing so turns (2) into
\[p\diamond(q\diamond p)=q,\]
which is exactly the desired conclusion.

Therefore the relation \(p\diamond(q\diamond p)=q\) holds for all \(p,q\in\Omega\), completing the proof.

core_steps: Substitute a ← (b * a) in (x*y)*x = y to obtain ((b*a)*b)*(b*a) = b
Swap the roles of a and b in the original identity to get (b*a)*b = a
Insert (b*a)*b = a into the first equation, yielding a*(b*a) = b

slot2.description: symbol chosen for the binary operation

slot3.description: names of the two generic elements

slot4.description: composite element used in the substitution step

slot5.description: direction of variable swap when re-applying the identity
